1.author: Ahmed Ali
1.text: ALIF LAM RA. These are the verses of the immaculate Book.

1.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
1.text: Alif-Lam-Ra*; these are verses of the clear Book. (* Alphabets of the Arabic language; Allah and to whomever He reveals, know their precise meanings.)

1.author: Arberry
1.text: Alif Lam Ra. Those are the signs of the Manifest Book.

1.author: Daryabadi
1.text: Alif. Lam. Ra. These are the verses of a Book luminous.

1.author: Hilali & Khan
1.text: Alif-Lam-Ra. [These letters are one of the miracles of the Quran, and none but Allah (Alone) knows their meanings]. These are the Verses of the Clear Book (the Quran that makes clear the legal and illegal things, legal laws, a guidance and a blessing).

1.author: Itani
1.text: Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the Verses of the Clear Book.

1.author: Maududi
1.text: Alif. Lam. Ra'. These are the verses of a Book that clearly expounds the truth.

1.author: Mubarakpuri
1.text: Alif-Lam-Ra. These are the verses of the Book that is clear.

1.author: Pickthall
1.text: Alif. Lam. Ra. These are verse of the Scripture that maketh plain.

1.author: Qarai
1.text: Aif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Manifest Book.

1.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
1.text: AlifLaamRa. Those are the verses of the Clear Book.

1.author: Saheeh International
1.text: Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the verses of the clear Book.

1.author: Sarwar
1.text: Alif. Lam. Ra. These are the verses of the illustrious Book.

1.author: Shakir
1.text: Alif Lam Ra. These are the verses of the Book that makes (things) manifest.

1.author: Wahiduddin Khan
1.text: Alif Lam Ra. These are verses from the clear Book.

1.author: Yusuf Ali
1.text: A. L. R. These are the symbols (or Verses) of the perspicuous Book.

2.author: Ahmed Ali
2.text: We have sent it down as a clear discourse that you may understand.

2.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
2.text: Indeed We have sent down an Arabic Qur’an, so that you may perceive.

2.author: Arberry
2.text: We have sent it down as an Arabic Koran; haply you will understand.

2.author: Daryabadi
2.text: Verily We! We have sent it down, an Arabic Recitation, that haply ye may reflect.

2.author: Hilali & Khan
2.text: Verily, We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran in order that you may understand.

2.author: Itani
2.text: We have revealed it an Arabic Quran, so that you may understand.

2.author: Maududi
2.text: We have revealed it as a Recitation in Arabic that you may fully understand.

2.author: Mubarakpuri
2.text: Verily, We have sent it down as an Arabic Qur'an in order that you may understand.

2.author: Pickthall
2.text: Lo! We have revealed it, a Lecture in Arabic, that ye may understand.

2.author: Qarai
2.text: Indeed We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason.

2.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
2.text: We have sent it down, an Arabic Koran, in order that you understand.

2.author: Saheeh International
2.text: Indeed, We have sent it down as an Arabic Qur'an that you might understand.

2.author: Sarwar
2.text: We have revealed it in the Arabic language so that you (people) would understand it.

2.author: Shakir
2.text: Surely We have revealed it-- an Arabic Quran-- that you may understand.

2.author: Wahiduddin Khan
2.text: We have sent down the Quran in Arabic, so that you may understand.

2.author: Yusuf Ali
2.text: We have sent it down as an Arabic Qur'an, in order that ye may learn wisdom.

2.author: Transliteration
2.text: Inna anzalnahu quranan AAarabiyyan laAAallakum taAAqiloona

3.author: Ahmed Ali
3.text: Through the revelation of this Qur'an We narrate the best of histories of which you were unaware before.

3.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
3.text: We relate to you the best narrative because We have sent the divine revelation of this Qur’an, to you; although surely you were unaware before this.

3.author: Arberry
3.text: We will relate to thee the fairest of stories in that We have revealed to thee this Koran, though before it thou wast one of the heedless.

3.author: Daryabadi
3.text: We! We recount unto thee the best of stories, by Revealing unto thee this Our'an, although thou wast before that of the unaware ones.

3.author: Hilali & Khan
3.text: We relate unto you (Muhammad SAW) the best of stories through Our Revelations unto you, of this Quran. And before this (i.e. before the coming of Divine Inspiration to you), you were among those who knew nothing about it (the Quran).

3.author: Itani
3.text: We narrate to you the most accurate history, by revealing to you this Quran. Although, prior to it, you were of the unaware.

3.author: Maududi
3.text: (O Muhammad), by revealing the Qur'an to you We narrate to you in the best manner the stories of the past although before this narration you were utterly unaware of them.

3.author: Mubarakpuri
3.text: We relate unto you the best of stories through Our revelations unto you, of this Qur'an. And before this, you were among those who knew nothing about it.

3.author: Pickthall
3.text: We narrate unto thee (Muhammad) the best of narratives in that We have inspired in thee this Qur'an, though aforetime thou wast of the heedless.

3.author: Qarai
3.text: We will recount to you the best of narratives in what We have revealed to you of this Quran, and indeed prior to it you were among those who are unaware [of it].

3.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
3.text: In the sending down of this Koran, We will narrate to you (Prophet Muhammad) the best of narratives, of which you were previously unaware.

3.author: Saheeh International
3.text: We relate to you, [O Muhammad], the best of stories in what We have revealed to you of this Qur'an although you were, before it, among the unaware.

3.author: Sarwar
3.text: In revealing this Quran to you, We tell you the best of the stories of which you were unaware.

3.author: Shakir
3.text: We narrate to you the best of narratives, by Our revealing to you this Quran, though before this you were certainly one of those who did not know.

3.author: Wahiduddin Khan
3.text: We recount to you the best of narratives in revealing this Quran to you, even though you were unaware of it before it came.

3.author: Yusuf Ali
3.text: We do relate unto thee the most beautiful of stories, in that We reveal to thee this (portion of the) Qur'an: before this, thou too was among those who knew it not.

3.author: Transliteration
3.text: Nahnu naqussu AAalayka ahsana alqasasi bima awhayna ilayka hatha alqurana wain kunta min qablihi lamina alghafileena

4.author: Ahmed Ali
4.text: When Joseph told his father: "O my father, I saw eleven stars and the sun and the moon bowing before me in homage,"

4.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
4.text: Remember when Yusuf (Joseph) said to his father, “O my father! I saw eleven stars and the sun and the moon – I saw them prostrating to me.”

4.author: Arberry
4.text: When Joseph said to his father, 'Father, I saw eleven stars, and the sun and the moon; I saw them bowing down before me.'

4.author: Daryabadi
4.text: Recall what time Yusufj said unto his father:, y father! verily have seen eleven stars and the sun and the moon; I have seen them prostrating themselves unto me.

4.author: Hilali & Khan
4.text: (Remember) when Yusuf (Joseph) said to his father: "O my father! Verily, I saw (in a dream) eleven stars and the sun and the moon, I saw them prostrating themselves to me."

4.author: Itani
4.text: When Joseph said to his father, “O my father, I saw eleven planets, and the sun, and the moon; I saw them bowing down to me.”

4.author: Maududi
4.text: Call to mind when Joseph said to his father: "My father! I saw (in a dream) eleven stars and the sun and the moon: I saw them prostrating themselves before me."

4.author: Mubarakpuri
4.text: (Remember) when Yusuf said to his father: "O my father! Verily, I saw (in a dream) eleven stars and the sun and the moon - I saw them prostrating themselves to me."

4.author: Pickthall
4.text: When Joseph said unto his father: O my father! Lo! I saw in a dream eleven planets and the sun and the moon, I saw them prostrating themselves unto me.

4.author: Qarai
4.text: When Joseph said to his father, ‘Father! I saw eleven planets, and the sun and the moon: I saw them prostrating themselves before me,’

4.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
4.text: When Joseph said to his father: 'Father, I saw eleven planets, and the sun and the moon; I saw them prostrating themselves before me'

4.author: Saheeh International
4.text: [Of these stories mention] when Joseph said to his father, "O my father, indeed I have seen [in a dream] eleven stars and the sun and the moon; I saw them prostrating to me."

4.author: Sarwar
4.text: When Joseph said, "Father, in my dream I saw eleven stars, the sun and the moon prostrating before me,"

4.author: Shakir
4.text: When Yusuf said to his father: O my father! surely I saw eleven stars and the sun and the moon-- I saw them making obeisance to me.

4.author: Wahiduddin Khan
4.text: When Joseph told his father, "My father, I saw eleven stars, and the sun and moon: I saw them prostrate themselves before me,"

4.author: Yusuf Ali
4.text: Behold! Joseph said to his father: "O my father! I did see eleven stars and the sun and the moon: I saw them prostrate themselves to me!"

4.author: Transliteration
4.text: Ith qala yoosufu liabeehi ya abati innee raaytu ahada AAashara kawkaban waalshshamsa waalqamara raaytuhum lee sajideena

5.author: Ahmed Ali
5.text: He said: "O son, do not narrate your dream to your brothers, or they will plot against you. Surely Satan is man's acknowledged foe.

5.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
5.text: He said, “O my child! Do not relate your dream to your brothers, for they will hatch a plot against you; indeed Satan is an open enemy towards mankind.”

5.author: Arberry
5.text: He said, 'O my son, relate not thy vision to thy brothers, lest they devise against thee some guile. Surely Satan is to man a manifest enemy.

5.author: Daryabadi
5.text: He said: O my son! recount not thine vision unto thy brethren, lest they plot a plot against thee; verily the Satan is unto man an enemy manifest.

5.author: Hilali & Khan
5.text: He (the father) said: "O my son! Relate not your vision to your brothers, lest they arrange a plot against you. Verily! Shaitan (Satan) is to man an open enemy!

5.author: Itani
5.text: He said, “O my son, do not relate your vision to your brothers, lest they plot and scheme against you. Satan is man's sworn enemy.

5.author: Maududi
5.text: His father said: "My son! Do not relate your dream to your brothers lest they hatch a plot to harm you. Indeed Satan is man's open enemy.

5.author: Mubarakpuri
5.text: He (the father) said: "O my son! Relate not your vision to your brothers, lest they should arrange a plot against you. Verily, Shaytan is to man an open enemy!"

5.author: Pickthall
5.text: He said: O my dear son! Tell not thy brethren of thy vision, lest they plot a plot against thee. Lo! Satan is for man an open foe.

5.author: Qarai
5.text: he said, ‘My son, do not recount your dream to your brothers, lest they should devise schemes against you. Satan is indeed man’s manifest enemy.

5.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
5.text: He said: 'O my son, say nothing of this vision to your brothers lest they should cunningly plot against you indeed, satan is the clear enemy of the human,

5.author: Saheeh International
5.text: He said, "O my son, do not relate your vision to your brothers or they will contrive against you a plan. Indeed Satan, to man, is a manifest enemy.

5.author: Sarwar
5.text: his father said, "My son, do not tell your dream to your brothers lest they plot against you; satan is the sworn enemy of man.

5.author: Shakir
5.text: He said: O my son! do not relate your vision to your brothers, lest they devise a plan against you; surely the Shaitan is an open enemy to man.

5.author: Wahiduddin Khan
5.text: he replied, "My son, do not relate your dream to your brothers, lest they plot evil against you -- Satan is the sworn enemy of man.

5.author: Yusuf Ali
5.text: Said (the father): "My (dear) little son! relate not thy vision to thy brothers, lest they concoct a plot against thee: for Satan is to man an avowed enemy!

5.author: Transliteration
5.text: Qala ya bunayya la taqsus ruyaka AAala ikhwatika fayakeedoo laka kaydan inna alshshaytana lilinsani AAaduwwun mubeenun

6.author: Ahmed Ali
6.text: Your Lord will choose you and teach you to interpret events, and confer His favours on you and the house of Jacob, as He had done in the case of two ancestors of yours, Abraham and Isaac, before you. Indeed your Lord is all-knowing and all-wise."

6.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
6.text: “And this is how your Lord will choose you and teach you how to interpret events, and will perfect His favours upon you and upon the family of Yaqub, the way He perfected it upon both your forefathers, Ibrahim and Ishaq; indeed your Lord is All Knowing, Wise.”

6.author: Arberry
6.text: So will thy Lord choose thee, and teach thee the interpretation of tales, and perfect His blessing upon thee and upon the House of Jacob, as He perfected it formerly on thy fathers Abraham and Isaac; surely thy Lord is All-knowing, All-wise.'

6.author: Daryabadi
6.text: And Thus will thy Lord choose thee and teach thee of the interpretation of discourses, and will fulfil His favour upon thee and upon the house of Y'aqub even as He fulfilled it upon thy fathers, Ibrahim and Is-haq aforetime; verily thy Lord is Knowing, Wise.

6.author: Hilali & Khan
6.text: "Thus will your Lord choose you and teach you the interpretation of dreams (and other things) and perfect His Favour on you and on the offspring of Ya'qub (Jacob), as He perfected it on your fathers, Ibrahim (Abraham) and Ishaque (Isaac) aforetime! Verily, your Lord is All-Knowing, All-Wise."

6.author: Itani
6.text: And thus your Lord will choose you, and will teach you the interpretation of events, and will complete His blessing upon you and upon the family of Jacob, as He has completed it before upon your forefathers Abraham and Isaac. Your Lord is Knowing and Wise.

6.author: Maududi
6.text: (As you have seen in the dream), so will your Lord choose you (for His task) and will impart to you the comprehension of the deeper meaning of things and will bestow the full measure of His favour upon you and upon the house of Jacob even as He earlier bestowed it in full measure upon your forefathers, Abraham and Isaac. Surely your Lord is All- Knowing, All-Wise."

6.author: Mubarakpuri
6.text: "Thus will your Lord choose you and teach you the interpretation of dreams (and other things) and perfect His favor on you and on the offspring of Ya`qub, as He perfected it on your fathers, Ibrahim and Ishaq aforetime! Verily, your Lord is All-Knowing, All-Wise."

6.author: Pickthall
6.text: Thus thy Lord will prefer thee and will teach thee the interpretation of events, and will perfect His grace upon thee and upon the family of Jacob as He perfected it upon thy forefathers, Abraham and Isaac. Lo! thy Lord is Knower, Wise.

6.author: Qarai
6.text: That is how your Lord will choose you, and teach you the interpretation of dreams, and complete His blessing upon you and upon the house of Jacob, just as He completed it earlier for your fathers, Abraham and Isaac. Your Lord is indeed all-knowing and all-wise.’

6.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
6.text: so your Lord will choose you and teach you the interpretation of visions, and perfect His Favor upon you and upon the House of Jacob, as He perfected it on your fathers Abraham and Isaac before you. Your Lord is Knowing, Wise.

6.author: Saheeh International
6.text: And thus will your Lord choose you and teach you the interpretation of narratives and complete His favor upon you and upon the family of Jacob, as He completed it upon your fathers before, Abraham and Isaac. Indeed, your Lord is Knowing and Wise."

6.author: Sarwar
6.text: Thus, your Lord will select you, teach you the interpretation of dreams, and grant His favors to you and the family of Jacob, just as He granted His favors to your fathers, Abraham and Isaac. Your Lord is certainly All-knowing and All-wise."

6.author: Shakir
6.text: And thus will your Lord choose you and teach you the interpretation of sayings and make His favor complete to you and to the children of Yaqoub, as He made it complete before to your fathers, Ibrahim and Ishaq; surely your Lord is Knowing, Wise.

6.author: Wahiduddin Khan
6.text: You shall be chosen by your Lord and He will impart to you some understanding of the inner meaning of events. He will bestow the full measure of His blessings upon you and upon the House of Jacob -- even as He formerly bestowed it in full measure upon your forefathers, Abraham and Isaac. Truly, your Sustainer is all-knowing and wise!"

6.author: Yusuf Ali
6.text: "Thus will thy Lord choose thee and teach thee the interpretation of stories (and events) and perfect His favour to thee and to the posterity of Jacob - even as He perfected it to thy fathers Abraham and Isaac aforetime! for Allah is full of knowledge and wisdom."

6.author: Transliteration
6.text: Wakathalika yajtabeeka rabbuka wayuAAallimuka min taweeli alahadeethi wayutimmu niAAmatahu AAalayka waAAala ali yaAAqooba kama atammaha AAala abawayka min qablu ibraheema waishaqa inna rabbaka AAaleemun hakeemun

7.author: Ahmed Ali
7.text: In the story of Joseph and his brothers are lessons for those who inquire.

7.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
7.text: Indeed in Yusuf and his brothers are signs* for those who enquire**. (* Of the truthfulness of Prophet Mohammed – peace and blessings be upon him. ** The Jews who enquired about their story.)

7.author: Arberry
7.text: (In Joseph and his brethren were signs for those who ask questions.)

7.author: Daryabadi
7.text: Assuredly in Yusuf and his brethren there have been signs for the inquirers.

7.author: Hilali & Khan
7.text: Verily, in Yusuf (Joseph) and his brethren, there were Ayat (proofs, evidences, verses, lessons, signs, revelations, etc.) for those who ask.

7.author: Itani
7.text: In Joseph and his brothers are lessons for the seekers.

7.author: Maududi
7.text: Verily in the story of Joseph and his brothers there are many signs for those who inquire (about the truth).

7.author: Mubarakpuri
7.text: Verily, in Yusuf and his brethren there were Ayat for those who ask.

7.author: Pickthall
7.text: Verily in Joseph and his brethren are signs (of Allah's Sovereignty) for the inquiring.

7.author: Qarai
7.text: In Joseph and his brothers there are certainly signs for the seekers.

7.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
7.text: Indeed, in Joseph and his brothers there were signs for those who inquire.

7.author: Saheeh International
7.text: Certainly were there in Joseph and his brothers signs for those who ask,

7.author: Sarwar
7.text: In the story of Joseph and his brothers, there is evidence (of the truth) for those who seek to know.

7.author: Shakir
7.text: Certainly in Yusuf and his brothers there are signs for the inquirers.

7.author: Wahiduddin Khan
7.text: Surely, in Joseph and his brothers there are signs for the inquirers.

7.author: Yusuf Ali
7.text: Verily in Joseph and his brethren are signs (or symbols) for seekers (after Truth).

7.author: Transliteration
7.text: Laqad kana fee yoosufa waikhwatihi ayatun lilssaileena

8.author: Ahmed Ali
8.text: "Surely Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than we," (said his half brothers) "even though we are a well-knit band. Our father is surely in the wrong.

8.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
8.text: When they said, “Indeed Yusuf and his brother* are dearer to our father than we are, and we are one group; undoubtedly our father is, clearly, deeply engrossed in love.” (* Of the same mother.)

8.author: Arberry
8.text: When they said, 'Surely Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than we, though we are a band. Surely our father is in manifest error.

8.author: Daryabadi
8.text: Recall what time they said: surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we, whereas we are company; verily our father is in error manifest.

8.author: Hilali & Khan
8.text: When they said: "Truly, Yusuf (Joseph) and his brother (Benjamin) are loved more by our father than we, but we are 'Usbah (a strong group). Really, our father is in a plain error.

8.author: Itani
8.text: When they said, “Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, although we are a whole group. Our father is obviously in the wrong.

8.author: Maududi
8.text: And call to mind when the brothers of Joseph conferred together and said: "Surely Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, although we are a group of so many. Our father is clearly mistaken.

8.author: Mubarakpuri
8.text: When they said: "Truly, Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we, while we are `Usbah (a group). Really, our father is in a plain error."

8.author: Pickthall
8.text: When they said: Verily Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, many though we be. Lo! our father is in plain aberration.

8.author: Qarai
8.text: When they said, ‘Surely Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than [the rest of] us, though we are a hardy band. Our father is indeed in manifest error.’

8.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
8.text: They said: 'Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than ourselves, even though we are many. Truly, our father is obviously wrong.

8.author: Saheeh International
8.text: When they said, "Joseph and his brother are more beloved to our father than we, while we are a clan. Indeed, our father is in clear error.

8.author: Sarwar
8.text: Joseph's brothers said to one another, "There is no doubt that Joseph and his brother are more loved by our father, even though we are all his offspring. He (our father) is certainly in manifest error."

8.author: Shakir
8.text: When they said: Certainly Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we, though we are a (stronger) company; most surely our father is in manifest error:

8.author: Wahiduddin Khan
8.text: They said [to each other], "Surely Joseph and his brother [Benjamin] are dearer to our father than ourselves, although we are a band. Truly, our father is clearly mistaken.

8.author: Yusuf Ali
8.text: They said: "Truly Joseph and his brother are loved more by our father than we: But we are a goodly body! really our father is obviously wandering (in his mind)!

8.author: Transliteration
8.text: Ith qaloo layoosufu waakhoohu ahabbu ila abeena minna wanahnu AAusbatun inna abana lafee dalalin mubeenin

9.author: Ahmed Ali
9.text: Let us kill Joseph or cast him in some distant land so that we may get our father's exclusive affection; then play innocent."

9.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
9.text: “Kill Yusuf or throw him somewhere in the land, so that your father’s attention may be directed only towards you, and then after it you may again become righteous!”

9.author: Arberry
9.text: Kill you Joseph, or cast him forth into some land, that your father's face may be free for you, and thereafter you may be a righteous people.'

9.author: Daryabadi
9.text: Slay Yusuf or cast him forth to some land; your father's countenance will be free for you, and ye shall be thereafter a people favoured.

9.author: Hilali & Khan
9.text: "Kill Yusuf (Joseph) or cast him out to some (other) land, so that the favour of your father may be given to you alone, and after that you will be righteous folk (by intending repentance before committing the sin)."

9.author: Itani
9.text: “Kill Joseph, or throw him somewhere in the land, and your father‘s attention will be yours. Afterwards, you will be decent people.”

9.author: Maududi
9.text: So either kill Joseph or cast him into some distant land so that your father's attention may become exclusively yours. And after so doing become righteous."

9.author: Mubarakpuri
9.text: "Kill Yusuf or cast him out to some (other) land, so that the favor of your father may be given to you alone, and after that you will be righteous folk."

9.author: Pickthall
9.text: (One said): Kill Joseph or cast him to some (other) land, so that your father's favour may be all for you, and (that) ye may afterward be righteous folk.

9.author: Qarai
9.text: ‘Kill Joseph or cast him away into some [distant] land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively towards you, and thereafter you may become a righteous lot.’

9.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
9.text: (Let us) kill Joseph, or cast him away in some (far off) land, so that your father's face will be left for you, and afterwards you will be a righteous nation'

9.author: Saheeh International
9.text: Kill Joseph or cast him out to [another] land; the countenance of your father will [then] be only for you, and you will be after that a righteous people."

9.author: Sarwar
9.text: Some of them suggested, "Let us kill Joseph or leave him somewhere far away from the presence of our father. Only then shall we receive equal treatment and thereafter can become righteous people".

9.author: Shakir
9.text: Slay Yusuf or cast him (forth) into some land, so that your father's regard may be exclusively for you, and after that you may be a righteous people.

9.author: Wahiduddin Khan
9.text: Therefore, let us put Joseph to death or cast him away to some [far-off] land, so that our father's attention should turn only to us, and you can thereafter become a righteous people."

9.author: Yusuf Ali
9.text: "Slay ye Joseph or cast him out to some (unknown) land, that so the favour of your father may be given to you alone: (there will be time enough) for you to be righteous after that!"

9.author: Transliteration
9.text: Oqtuloo yoosufa awi itrahoohu ardan yakhlu lakum wajhu abeekum watakoonoo min baAAdihi qawman saliheena

10.author: Ahmed Ali
10.text: One of them said: "If you must do so, then do not kill Joseph, but throw him into an unused well. Some passing caravan may rescue him."

10.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
10.text: A speaker among them said, “Do not kill Yusuf – and drop him into a dark well so that some traveller may come and take him away, if you have to.”

10.author: Arberry
10.text: One of them said, 'No, kill not Joseph, but cast him into the bottom of the pit and some traveller will pick him out, if you do aught.'

10.author: Daryabadi
10.text: Said a speaker from among them; slay not Yusuf, but cast him into the bottom of a well, some of the caravan will take him up--if ye must be doing.

10.author: Hilali & Khan
10.text: One from among them said: "Kill not Yusuf (Joseph), but if you must do something, throw him down to the bottom of a well, he will be picked up by some caravan of travellers."

10.author: Itani
10.text: One of them said, “Do not kill Joseph, but throw him into the bottom of the well; some caravan may pick him up—if you must do something.”

10.author: Maududi
10.text: Thereupon one of them said: "Do not kill Joseph, but if you are bent upon doing something, cast him down to the bottom of some dark pit, perhaps some caravan passing by will take him out of it."

10.author: Mubarakpuri
10.text: One from among them said: "Kill not Yusuf, but if you must do something, throw him down to the bottom of a well; he will be picked up by some caravan of travelers."

10.author: Pickthall
10.text: One among them said: Kill not Joseph but, if ye must be doing, fling him into the depth of the pit; some caravan will find him.

10.author: Qarai
10.text: One of them said, ‘Do not kill Joseph, but cast him into the recess of a well so that some caravan may pick him up, if you are to do [anything].’

10.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
10.text: One of them said: 'No, do not kill Joseph, if you do anything, cast him into a dark pit, a traveler will pick him up'

10.author: Saheeh International
10.text: Said a speaker among them, "Do not kill Joseph but throw him into the bottom of the well; some travelers will pick him up - if you would do [something]."

10.author: Sarwar
10.text: One of them said, "Do not kill Joseph, but if you must, throw him into a dark well so that perhaps some caravan will take him away."

10.author: Shakir
10.text: A speaker from among them said: Do not slay Yusuf, and cast him down into the bottom of the pit if you must do (it), (so that) some of the travellers may pick him up.

10.author: Wahiduddin Khan
10.text: One of them said, "Do not kill Joseph, but if you must do something, cast him into the bottom of a well; some of the travellers will pick him up."

10.author: Yusuf Ali
10.text: Said one of them: "Slay not Joseph, but if ye must do something, throw him down to the bottom of the well: he will be picked up by some caravan of travellers."

10.author: Transliteration
10.text: Qala qailun minhum la taqtuloo yoosufa waalqoohu fee ghayabati aljubbi yaltaqithu baAAdu alssayyarati in kuntum faAAileena

11.author: Ahmed Ali
11.text: (Then going to their father) they said: "O father, why don't you trust us with Joseph? We are in fact his well-wishers.

11.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
11.text: They said, “O our father! What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf, although we are in fact his well-wishers?”

11.author: Arberry
11.text: They said, 'Father, what ails thee, that thou trustest us not with Joseph? Surely we are his sincere well-wishers.

11.author: Daryabadi
11.text: They said: our father! wherefore thou intrustest us not with Yusuf, whereas verily we are his well-wishers.

11.author: Hilali & Khan
11.text: They said: "O our father! Why do you not trust us with Yusuf (Joseph), - when we are indeed his well-wishers?"

11.author: Itani
11.text: They said, “Father, why do you not trust us with Joseph, although we care for him?”

11.author: Maududi
11.text: After so deciding they said to their father: "Why is it that you do not trust us regarding Joseph although we are his true well-wishers?"

11.author: Mubarakpuri
11.text: They said: "O our father! Why do you not trust us with Yusuf though we are indeed his well-wishers"

11.author: Pickthall
11.text: They said: O our father! Why wilt thou not trust us with Joseph, when lo! we are good friends to him?

11.author: Qarai
11.text: They said, ‘Father! Why is it that you do not trust us with Joseph? We are indeed his well-wishers.

11.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
11.text: They said: 'Father, what is the matter with you, do you not trust us with Joseph? Indeed, we are sincere advisors.

11.author: Saheeh International
11.text: They said, "O our father, why do you not entrust us with Joseph while indeed, we are to him sincere counselors?

11.author: Sarwar
11.text: Then they asked their father, "Why do you not trust us with Joseph? We are his well-wishers.

11.author: Shakir
11.text: They said: O our father! what reason have you that you do not trust in us with respect to Yusuf? And most surely we are his sincere well-wishers:

11.author: Wahiduddin Khan
11.text: They said to their father, "Why do you not trust us with Joseph? We are indeed his well-wishers.

11.author: Yusuf Ali
11.text: They said: "O our father! why dost thou not trust us with Joseph,- seeing we are indeed his sincere well-wishers?

11.author: Transliteration
11.text: Qaloo ya abana ma laka la tamanna AAala yoosufa wainna lahu lanasihoona

12.author: Ahmed Ali
12.text: Let him go out with us tomorrow that he may enjoy and play. We shall take care of him."

12.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
12.text: “Send him with us tomorrow so that he may eat some fruits and play, and indeed we are his protectors.”

12.author: Arberry
12.text: Send him forth with us tomorrow, to frolic and play; surely we shall be watching over him.'

12.author: Daryabadi
12.text: Send him with us tomorrow, that he may refresh himself and play, and verily we are to be his guards.

12.author: Hilali & Khan
12.text: "Send him with us tomorrow to enjoy himself and play, and verily, we will take care of him."

12.author: Itani
12.text: “Send him with us tomorrow, that he may roam and play; we will take care of him.”

12.author: Maududi
12.text: Send him out with us tomorrow that he may enjoy himself and play while we will be there, standing guard over him."

12.author: Mubarakpuri
12.text: "Send him with us tomorrow to enjoy himself and play, and verily, we will take care of him."

12.author: Pickthall
12.text: Send him with us to-morrow that he may enjoy himself and play. And lo! we shall take good care of him.

12.author: Qarai
12.text: Let him go with us tomorrow so that he may eat lots of fruits and play, and we will indeed take [good] care of him.’

12.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
12.text: Send him with us tomorrow to frolic and play. We will look after him'

12.author: Saheeh International
12.text: Send him with us tomorrow that he may eat well and play. And indeed, we will be his guardians.

12.author: Sarwar
12.text: Send him with us tomorrow to play with us and enjoy himself. We shall carefully protect him".

12.author: Shakir
12.text: Send him with us tomorrow that he may enjoy himself and sport, and surely we will guard him well.

12.author: Wahiduddin Khan
12.text: Send him with us tomorrow, so that he may play and enjoy himself. We will look after him."

12.author: Yusuf Ali
12.text: "Send him with us tomorrow to enjoy himself and play, and we shall take every care of him."

12.author: Transliteration
12.text: Arsilhu maAAana ghadan yartaAA wayalAAab wainna lahu lahafithoona

13.author: Ahmed Ali
13.text: He said: "I am afraid of sending him with you lest a wolf should devour him when you are unmindful."

13.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
13.text: He said, “I will indeed be saddened by your taking him away, and I fear that the wolf may devour him, whilst you are unaware of him.” (* Prophet Yaqub knew of what was about to happen.)

13.author: Arberry
13.text: He said, 'It grieves me that you should go with him, and I fear the wolf may eat him, while you are heedless of him.'

13.author: Daryabadi
13.text: He said: verily it grieveth me that ye should take him away, and I fear lest a wolf may devour him, While ye are negligent of him.

13.author: Hilali & Khan
13.text: He [Ya'qub (Jacob)] said: "Truly, it saddens me that you should take him away. I fear lest a wolf should devour him, while you are careless of him."

13.author: Itani
13.text: He said, “It worries me that you would take him away. And I fear the wolf may eat him while you are careless of him.”

13.author: Maududi
13.text: Their father answered: "It grieves me indeed that you should take him with you for I fear that some wolf might eat him up while you are negligent of him."

13.author: Mubarakpuri
13.text: He (Ya`qub) said: "Truly, it saddens me that you should take him away. I fear lest a wolf should devour him, while you are careless of him."

13.author: Pickthall
13.text: He said: Lo! in truth it saddens me that ye should take him with you, and I fear less the wolf devour him while ye are heedless of him.

13.author: Qarai
13.text: He said, ‘It really upsets me that you should take him away, and I fear the wolf may eat him while you are oblivious of him.’

13.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
13.text: He said: 'It grieves me to let him go with you, for I fear lest the wolf should devour him when you are not paying attention to him'

13.author: Saheeh International
13.text: [Jacob] said, "Indeed, it saddens me that you should take him, and I fear that a wolf would eat him while you are of him unaware."

13.author: Sarwar
13.text: Jacob replied, "I shall be grieved if you take him with you; I fear that some wild-beast will harm him in your absence."

13.author: Shakir
13.text: He said: Surely it grieves me that you should take him off, and I fear lest the wolf devour him while you are heedless of him.

13.author: Wahiduddin Khan
13.text: [Jacob] said, "It would indeed grieve me if you took him away with you, and I fear lest the wolf should devour him while you are off your guard."

13.author: Yusuf Ali
13.text: (Jacob) said: "Really it saddens me that ye should take him away: I fear lest the wolf should devour him while ye attend not to him."

13.author: Transliteration
13.text: Qala innee layahzununee an thathhaboo bihi waakhafu an yakulahu alththibu waantum AAanhu ghafiloona

14.author: Ahmed Ali
14.text: They replied: "If a wolf should devour him when we are there, a well-knit band, we shall certainly be treacherous."

14.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
14.text: They said, “If the wolf devours him, whereas we are a group, then surely we are useless!”

14.author: Arberry
14.text: They said, 'If the wolf eats him, and we a band, then are we losers!'

14.author: Daryabadi
14.text: They said: if the wolf devoured him while we were a company, we must indeed then be the losers!

14.author: Hilali & Khan
14.text: They said: "If a wolf devours him, while we are 'Usbah (a strong group) (to guard him), then surely, we are the losers."

14.author: Itani
14.text: They said, “If the wolf ate him, and we are many, we would be good for nothing.”

14.author: Maududi
14.text: They said: "Should a wolf eat him, despite the presence of our strong group, we would indeed be a worthless lot!"

14.author: Mubarakpuri
14.text: They said: "If a wolf devours him, while we are `Usbah (a group), then surely, we are the losers."

14.author: Pickthall
14.text: They said: If the wolf should devour him when we are (so strong) a band, then surely we should have already perished.

14.author: Qarai
14.text: They said, ‘Should the wolf eat him while we are a hardy band, then we will indeed be losers!’

14.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
14.text: They said: 'We are many, if a wolf devours him, then we are losers'

14.author: Saheeh International
14.text: They said, "If a wolf should eat him while we are a [strong] clan, indeed, we would then be losers."

14.author: Sarwar
14.text: They said, "If some wild-beast would be able to harm him, despite the presence of our strong group, it would certainly be a great loss to us!"

14.author: Shakir
14.text: They said: Surely if the wolf should devour him notwithstanding that we are a (strong) company, we should then certainly be losers.

14.author: Wahiduddin Khan
14.text: They said, "Surely, if the wolf devoured him while we were a strong party, we should indeed be great losers."

14.author: Yusuf Ali
14.text: They said: "If the wolf were to devour him while we are (so large) a party, then should we indeed (first) have perished ourselves!"

14.author: Transliteration
14.text: Qaloo lain akalahu alththibu wanahnu AAusbatun inna ithan lakhasiroona

15.author: Ahmed Ali
15.text: So, when they took him out they planned to throw him into an unused well. We revealed to Joseph: "You will tell them (one day) of this deed when they will not apprehend it."

15.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
15.text: So when they took him away – and all of them agreed that they should drop him in the dark well; and We sent the divine revelation to him, “You will surely tell them of their deed at a time when they will not know.”

15.author: Arberry
15.text: So when they went with him, and agreed to put him in the bottom of the well, and We revealed to him, 'Thou shalt tell them of this their doing when they are unaware.'

15.author: Daryabadi
15.text: So when they took him away, and resolved to place him in the bottom of the well, We Revealed unto him: surely thou wilt declare unto them this their affair, while they shall perceive not.

15.author: Hilali & Khan
15.text: So, when they took him away, they all agreed to throw him down to the bottom of the well, and We inspired in him:"Indeed, you shall (one day) inform them of this their affair, when they know (you) not."

15.author: Itani
15.text: So they went away with him, and agreed to put him at the bottom of the well. And We inspired him, “You will inform them of this deed of theirs when they are unaware.”

15.author: Maududi
15.text: So when they went away with Joseph and decided to cast him in the bottom of the dark pit, We revealed to Joseph: "Surely a time will come when you will remind them of their deed. They know nothing about the consequence of what they are doing."

15.author: Mubarakpuri
15.text: So, when they took him away, they all agreed to throw him down to the bottom of the well, and We revealed to him: "Indeed, you shall (one day) inform them of this their affair, when they know (you) not."

15.author: Pickthall
15.text: Then, when they led him off, and were of one mind that they should place him in the depth of the pit, We inspired in him: Thou wilt tell them of this deed of theirs when they know (thee) not.

15.author: Qarai
15.text: So when they took him away and conspired to put him into the recess of a well, We revealed to him, ‘[A day will come when] you will surely inform them about this affair of theirs while they are not aware [of your identity].’

15.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
15.text: When they went with him, they agreed to put him in the bottom of a well. We revealed to him: 'You shall tell them of what they did when they are not aware (it is you)'

15.author: Saheeh International
15.text: So when they took him [out] and agreed to put him into the bottom of the well... But We inspired to him, "You will surely inform them [someday] about this affair of theirs while they do not perceive [your identity]."

15.author: Sarwar
15.text: When they took Joseph with them, they agreed to throw him into the well. We revealed to Joseph that (sometime) in the future at a time when they would not recognize him, he would remind them of all this.

15.author: Shakir
15.text: So when they had gone off with him and agreed that they should put him down at the bottom of the pit, and We revealed to him: You will most certainly inform them of this their affair while they do not perceive.

15.author: Wahiduddin Khan
15.text: And so, when they went away with him, they decided to cast him into the dark depths of a well. Then We revealed to him Our will, "You shall [one day] tell them of this deed of theirs, when they do not realize who you are."

15.author: Yusuf Ali
15.text: So they did take him away, and they all agreed to throw him down to the bottom of the well: and We put into his heart (this Message): 'Of a surety thou shalt (one day) tell them the truth of this their affair while they know (thee) not'

15.author: Transliteration
15.text: Falamma thahaboo bihi waajmaAAoo an yajAAaloohu fee ghayabati aljubbi waawhayna ilayhi latunabiannahum biamrihim hatha wahum la yashAAuroona

16.author: Ahmed Ali
16.text: At nightfall they came to their father weeping,

16.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
16.text: And at nightfall they came to their father, weeping.

16.author: Arberry
16.text: And they came to their father in the evening, and they were weeping.

16.author: Daryabadi
16.text: And they came to their father at nightfall, weeping.

16.author: Hilali & Khan
16.text: And they came to their father in the early part of the night weeping.

16.author: Itani
16.text: And they came to their father in the evening weeping.

16.author: Maududi
16.text: At nightfall they came to their father weeping

16.author: Mubarakpuri
16.text: And they came to their father in the early part of the night weeping.

16.author: Pickthall
16.text: And they came weeping to their father in the evening.

16.author: Qarai
16.text: In the evening, they came weeping to their father.

16.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
16.text: At nightfall, they returned weeping to their father.

16.author: Saheeh International
16.text: And they came to their father at night, weeping.

16.author: Sarwar
16.text: In the evening they returned to their father weeping

16.author: Shakir
16.text: And they came to their father at nightfall, weeping.

16.author: Wahiduddin Khan
16.text: And at nightfall they came to their father, weeping.

16.author: Yusuf Ali
16.text: Then they came to their father in the early part of the night, weeping.

17.author: Ahmed Ali
17.text: And said: "We went racing with one another and left Joseph to guard our things when a wolf devoured him. But you will not believe us even though we tell the truth."

17.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
17.text: Saying, “O our father! We went far ahead while racing, and left Yusuf near our resources – therefore the wolf devoured him; and you will not believe us although we may be truthful.”

17.author: Arberry
17.text: They said, 'Father, we went running races, and left Joseph behind with our things; so the wolf ate him. But thou wouldst never believe us, though we spoke truly.'

17.author: Daryabadi
17.text: They said: our father! we went off competing, and left Yusuf by our stuff, so a wolf devoured him; and thou wilt put no credence in us, even though we are the truth-tellers.

17.author: Hilali & Khan
17.text: They said:"O our father! We went racing with one another, and left Yusuf (Joseph) by our belongings and a wolf devoured him; but you will never believe us even when we speak the truth."

17.author: Itani
17.text: They said, “O father, we went off racing one another, and left Joseph by our belongings; and the wolf ate him. But you will not believe us, even though we are being truthful.”

17.author: Maududi
17.text: and said: "Father! We went racing with one another and left Joseph behind with our things, and then a wolf came and ate him up. We know that you will not believe us howsoever truthful we might be."

17.author: Mubarakpuri
17.text: They said: "O our father! We went racing with one another, and left Yusuf by our belongings and a wolf devoured him; but you will never believe us even when we speak the truth."

17.author: Pickthall
17.text: Saying: O our father! We went racing one with another, and left Joseph by our things, and the wolf devoured him, and thou believest not our saying even when we speak the truth.

17.author: Qarai
17.text: They said, ‘Father! We had gone racing and left Joseph with our things, whereat the wolf ate him. But you will not believe us even if we spoke truly.’

17.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
17.text: They said: 'We went racing and left Joseph with our things. The wolf devoured him, but you will not believe us, though we speak the truth'

17.author: Saheeh International
17.text: They said, "O our father, indeed we went racing each other and left Joseph with our possessions, and a wolf ate him. But you would not believe us, even if we were truthful."

17.author: Sarwar
17.text: and saying, "Father, we went playing and left Joseph with our belongings. A wild-beast came and devoured him. We realize that you will not believe us even though we are telling the truth".

17.author: Shakir
17.text: They said: O our father! surely we went off racing and left Yusuf by our goods, so the wolf devoured him, and you will not believe us though we are truthful.

17.author: Wahiduddin Khan
17.text: They said, "Father, we went off racing and left Joseph with our belongings, and the wolf devoured him. But you will not believe us, even though we are telling the truth."

17.author: Yusuf Ali
17.text: They said: "O our father! We went racing with one another, and left Joseph with our things; and the wolf devoured him.... But thou wilt never believe us even though we tell the truth."

17.author: Transliteration
17.text: Qaloo ya abana inna thahabna nastabiqu watarakna yoosufa AAinda mataAAina faakalahu alththibu wama anta bimuminin lana walaw kunna sadiqeena

18.author: Ahmed Ali
18.text: They showed him the shirt with false blood on it. (Their father) said: "It is not so; you have made up the story. Yet endurance is best. I seek the help of God alone for what you impute."

18.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
18.text: And they brought his shirt stained with faked blood; he said, “On the contrary – your hearts have fabricated an excuse for you; therefore patience is better; and from Allah only I seek help against the matters that you relate.”

18.author: Arberry
18.text: And they brought his shirt with false blood on it. He said, 'No; but your spirits tempted you to do somewhat. But come, sweet patience! And God's succour is ever there to seek against that you describe.'

18.author: Daryabadi
18.text: And they brought his shirt with false blood. He said: nay! your selves have embellished for you an affair; so seemly patience! and Allah is to be implored for help in that which ye ascribe

18.author: Hilali & Khan
18.text: And they brought his shirt stained with false blood. He said: "Nay, but your ownselves have made up a tale. So (for me) patience is most fitting. And it is Allah (Alone) Whose help can be sought against that which you assert."

18.author: Itani
18.text: And they brought his shirt, with fake blood on it. He said, “Your souls enticed you to do something. But patience is beautiful, and God is my Help against what you describe.”

18.author: Maududi
18.text: And they brought Joseph's shirt, stained with false blood. Seeing this their father exclaimed: "Nay (this is not true); rather your evil souls have made it easy for you to commit a heinous act. So I will bear this patiently, and in good grace. It is Allah's help alone that I seek against your fabrication."

18.author: Mubarakpuri
18.text: And they brought his shirt stained with false blood. He said: "Nay, but your own selves have made up a tale. So (for me) patience is most fitting. And it is Allah (Alone) Whose help can be sought against that (lie) which you describe."

18.author: Pickthall
18.text: And they came with false blood on his shirt. He said: Nay, but your minds have beguiled you into something. (My course is) comely patience. And Allah it is Whose help is to be sought in that (predicament) which ye describe.

18.author: Qarai
18.text: And they produced sham blood on his shirt. He said, ‘No, your souls have made a matter seem decorous to you. Yet patience is graceful, and Allah is my resort against what you allege.’

18.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
18.text: And they brought his shirt (stained) with blood, a lie. He said: 'No, your souls have tempted you to do something. But come sweet patience! The help ofAllah is always there to seek against that which you describe'

18.author: Saheeh International
18.text: And they brought upon his shirt false blood. [Jacob] said, "Rather, your souls have enticed you to something, so patience is most fitting. And Allah is the one sought for help against that which you describe."

18.author: Sarwar
18.text: They presented him with a shirt stained with false blood. Jacob said, "Your souls have tempted you in this matter. Let us be patient and beg assistance from God if what you say is true."

18.author: Shakir
18.text: And they brought his shirt with false blood upon it. He said: Nay, your souls have made the matter light for you, but patience is good and Allah is He Whose help is sought for against what you describe.

18.author: Wahiduddin Khan
18.text: And they showed him their brother's shirt, stained with false blood. "No!" he cried. "Your souls have tempted you to do something evil! But it is best to be patient: God alone can help me bear the loss you speak of."

18.author: Yusuf Ali
18.text: They stained his shirt with false blood. He said: "Nay, but your minds have made up a tale (that may pass) with you, (for me) patience is most fitting: Against that which ye assert, it is Allah (alone) Whose help can be sought"..

18.author: Transliteration
18.text: Wajaoo AAala qameesihi bidamin kathibin qala bal sawwalat lakum anfusukum amran fasabrun jameelun waAllahu almustaAAanu AAala ma tasifoona

19.author: Ahmed Ali
19.text: A caravan happened to pass, and sent the water-carrier to bring water from the well. He let down his bucket (and pulled Joseph up with it). "What luck," said the man; "here is a boy;" and they hid him as an item of merchandise; but what they did was known to God.

19.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
19.text: And there came a caravan – so they sent their water-drawer, he therefore lowered his pail; he said, “What good luck, this is a boy!”; and they hid him as a treasure; and Allah knows what they do.

19.author: Arberry
19.text: Then came travellers, and they sent one of them, a water-drawer, who let down his bucket. 'Good news!' he said. 'Here is a young man.' So they hid him as merchandise; but God knew what they were doing.

19.author: Daryabadi
19.text: And a caravan came, and they sent their water-drawer, and he let down his bucket. He said: glad tidings! here is a youth. And they hid him as merchandise, And Allah was the Knower of that which they worked.

19.author: Hilali & Khan
19.text: And there came a caravan of travellers; they sent their water-drawer, and he let down his bucket (into the well). He said: "What good news! Here is a boy." So they hid him as merchandise (a slave). And Allah was the All-Knower of what they did.

19.author: Itani
19.text: A caravan passed by, and they sent their water-carrier. He lowered his bucket, and said, “Good news. Here is a boy.” And they hid him as merchandise. But God was aware of what they did.

19.author: Maududi
19.text: And a caravan came, and they sent their water drawer to draw water. As he let down his bucket in the well he (observed Joseph) and cried out: "This is good news. There is a boy." They concealed him, considering him as part of their merchandise, while Allah was well aware of what they did.

19.author: Mubarakpuri
19.text: And there came a caravan of travelers and they sent their water-drawer, and he let down his bucket (into the well). He said: "What good news! Here is a boy." So they hid him as merchandise (a slave). And Allah was the All-Knower of what they did.

19.author: Pickthall
19.text: And there came a caravan, and they sent their waterdrawer. He let down his pail (into the pit). He said: Good luck! Here is a youth. And they hid him as a treasure, and Allah was Aware of what they did.

19.author: Qarai
19.text: There came a caravan, and they sent their water-drawer, who let down his bucket. ‘Good news!’ he said. ‘This is a young boy!’ So they hid him as [a piece of] merchandise, and Allah knew best what they were doing.

19.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
19.text: Thereafter travelers came, and sent their waterman. And when he had let down his pail, (he cried:) 'Rejoice, a boy' Then they concealed him among their merchandise, but Allah knew what they did.

19.author: Saheeh International
19.text: And there came a company of travelers; then they sent their water drawer, and he let down his bucket. He said, "Good news! Here is a boy." And they concealed him, [taking him] as merchandise; and Allah was knowing of what they did.

19.author: Sarwar
19.text: A caravan came by and sent their water carrier out to the well. When he drew out Joseph in his bucket, he shouted, "Glad news, a young boy!" The people of the caravan hid him amongst their belongings. God knows well what they do.

19.author: Shakir
19.text: And there came travellers and they sent their water-drawer and he let down his bucket. He said: O good news! this is a youth; and they concealed him as an article of merchandise, and Allah knew what they did.

19.author: Wahiduddin Khan
19.text: And there came a caravan of travellers and they sent their water-drawer to draw water from the well. He let down his bucket into the well and he exclaimed, "Oh, what a lucky find, here is a boy!" They hid him like a piece of merchandise, but God knew well what they did.

19.author: Yusuf Ali
19.text: Then there came a caravan of travellers: they sent their water-carrier (for water), and he let down his bucket (into the well)... He said: "Ah there! Good news! Here is a (fine) young man!" So they concealed him as a treasure! But Allah knoweth well all that they do!

19.author: Transliteration
19.text: Wajaat sayyaratun faarsaloo waridahum faadla dalwahu qala ya bushra hatha ghulamun waasarroohu bidaAAatan waAllahu AAaleemun bima yaAAmaloona

20.author: Ahmed Ali
20.text: And they sold him as worthless for a few paltry dirham.

20.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
20.text: And the brothers sold him for an improper price, a limited number of coins; and they had no interest in him.

20.author: Arberry
20.text: Then they sold him for a paltry price, a handful of counted dirhams; for they set small store by him.

20.author: Daryabadi
20.text: And they sold him for a mean price: a few dirhams numbered and they were in regard to him of the indifferent.

20.author: Hilali & Khan
20.text: And they sold him for a low price, - for a few Dirhams (i.e. for a few silver coins). And they were of those who regarded him insignificant.

20.author: Itani
20.text: And they sold him for a cheap price—a few coins—they considered him to be of little value.

20.author: Maududi
20.text: Later they sold him for a paltry sum - just a few dirhams; they did not care to obtain a higher price.

20.author: Mubarakpuri
20.text: And they sold him for a Bakhs price, - for a few Dirhams. And they were of those who regarded him insignificant.

20.author: Pickthall
20.text: And they sold him for a low price, a number of silver coins; and they attached no value to him.

20.author: Qarai
20.text: And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams, for they set small store by him.

20.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
20.text: Then, they sold him for a trifling price, a number of dirhams, because they considered him to be of little value.

20.author: Saheeh International
20.text: And they sold him for a reduced price - a few dirhams - and they were, concerning him, of those content with little.

20.author: Sarwar
20.text: In selling him they asked for a very small price and even then no one wanted to buy him.

20.author: Shakir
20.text: And they sold him for a small price, a few pieces of silver, and they showed no desire for him.

20.author: Wahiduddin Khan
20.text: Later they sold him for a paltry sum, a few pieces of silver [dirhams]: So little did they value him.

20.author: Yusuf Ali
20.text: The (Brethren) sold him for a miserable price, for a few dirhams counted out: in such low estimation did they hold him!

20.author: Transliteration
20.text: Washarawhu bithamanin bakhsin darahima maAAdoodatin wakanoo feehi mina alzzahideena

21.author: Ahmed Ali
21.text: The Egyptian who bought him instructed his wife: "House him honourably. He may be of use to us. We may even adopt him as a son." So, We firmly established Joseph in the land, and taught him the interpretation of dreams. God dominated in his affairs, though most men do not know.

21.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
21.text: And the Egyptian who purchased him said to his wife, “Keep him honourably – we may derive some benefit due to him or we may adopt him as our son”; and this is how we established Yusuf in the land, and that We might teach him how to interpret events; and Allah is Dominant upon His works, but most men do not know.

21.author: Arberry
21.text: He that bought him, being of Egypt, said to his wife, 'Give him goodly lodging, and it may be that he will profit us, or we may take him for our own son.' So We established Joseph in the land, and that We might teach him the interpretation of tales. God prevails in His purpose, but most men know not.

21.author: Daryabadi
21.text: And he who bought him in Misr said unto his wife: make his dwelling honourable: belike he may profit us or we may take him as a son. And Thus We made a place for Yusuf in the land, and it was in order that We may teach him the interpretation of discourses. And Allah is Dominant in His purpose, but most of men know not.

21.author: Hilali & Khan
21.text: And he (the man) from Egypt who bought him, said to his wife: "Make his stay comfortable, may be he will profit us or we shall adopt him as a son." Thus did We establish Yusuf (Joseph) in the land, that We might teach him the interpretation of events. And Allah has full power and control over His Affairs, but most of men know not.

21.author: Itani
21.text: The Egyptian who bought him said to his wife, “Take good care of him; he may be useful to us, or we may adopt him as a son.” We thus established Joseph in the land, to teach him the interpretation of events. God has control over His affairs, but most people do not know.

21.author: Maududi
21.text: The man from Egypt who bought him said to his wife: "Take good care of him, possibly he might be of benefit to us or we might adopt him as a son." Thus We found a way for Joseph to become established in that land and in order that We might teach him to comprehend the deeper meaning of things. Allah has full power to implement His design although most people do not know that.

21.author: Mubarakpuri
21.text: And he (the man) from Egypt who bought him, said to his wife: "Make his stay comfortable, maybe he will profit us or we shall adopt him as a son." Thus did We establish Yusuf in the land, that We might teach him the interpretation of events. And Allah has full power and control over His affairs, but most of men know not.

21.author: Pickthall
21.text: And he of Egypt who purchased him said unto his wife: Receive him honourably. Perchance he may prove useful to us or we may adopt him as a son. Thus we established Joseph in the land that We might teach him the interpretation of events. And Allah was predominant in His career, but most of mankind know not.

21.author: Qarai
21.text: The man from Egypt who had bought him said to his wife, ‘Give him an honourable place [in the household]. Maybe he will be useful to us, or we may adopt him as a son.’ Thus We established Joseph in the land and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams. Allah has [full] command of His affairs, but most people do not know.

21.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
21.text: The Egyptian that bought him said to his wife: 'Make his stay honorable. He may benefit us, or take him for our son' As such We established Joseph in the land, so that We might teach him the interpretation of visions. Allah prevails in His affairs, though most people do not know.

21.author: Saheeh International
21.text: And the one from Egypt who bought him said to his wife, "Make his residence comfortable. Perhaps he will benefit us, or we will adopt him as a son." And thus, We established Joseph in the land that We might teach him the interpretation of events. And Allah is predominant over His affair, but most of the people do not know.

21.author: Sarwar
21.text: The Egyptian who bought him said to his wife, "Be kind to him, perhaps he will be of some benefit to us or we may adopt him." Thus, We settled Joseph in the land so that We could teach him the interpretation of dreams. God has full control over His affairs but most people do not know.

21.author: Shakir
21.text: And the Egyptian who bought him said to his wife: Give him an honorable abode, maybe he will be useful to us, or we may adopt him as a son. And thus did We establish Yusuf in the land and that We might teach him the interpretation of sayings; and Allah is the master of His affair, but most people do not know.

21.author: Wahiduddin Khan
21.text: The Egyptian who bought him said to his wife, "Lodge him honourably, he may prove of benefit to us, or we may even adopt him as our son." Thus We established Joseph in the land, so that We might teach him the true meaning of events. God has power over all things. However, most people do not know this.

21.author: Yusuf Ali
21.text: The man in Egypt who bought him, said to his wife: "Make his stay (among us) honourable: may be he will bring us much good, or we shall adopt him as a son." Thus did We establish Joseph in the land, that We might teach him the interpretation of stories (and events). And Allah hath full power and control over His affairs; but most among mankind know it not.

21.author: Transliteration
21.text: Waqala allathee ishtarahu min misra liimraatihi akrimee mathwahu AAasa an yanfaAAana aw nattakhithahu waladan wakathalika makkanna liyoosufa fee alardi walinuAAallimahu min taweeli alahadeethi waAllahu ghalibun AAala amrihi walakinna akthara alnnasi la yaAAlamoona

22.author: Ahmed Ali
22.text: When he reached the prime of life We gave him wisdom and knowledge. Thus We reward those who are good.

22.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
22.text: And when he matured to his full strength, We bestowed him wisdom and knowledge; and this is how We reward the virtuous.

22.author: Arberry
22.text: And when he was fully grown, We gave him judgment and knowledge. Even so We recompense the good-doers.

22.author: Daryabadi
22.text: And when he reached his maturity We vouchsafed unto him judgement and knowledge; and Thus We recompense the well-doers.

22.author: Hilali & Khan
22.text: And when he [Yusuf (Joseph)] attained his full manhood, We gave him wisdom and knowledge (the Prophethood), thus We reward the Muhsinun (doers of good - see V. 2:112).

22.author: Itani
22.text: When he reached his maturity, We gave him wisdom and knowledge. We thus reward the righteous.

22.author: Maududi
22.text: And when Joseph reached the age of maturity, We granted him judgement and knowledge. Thus do We reward those who do good.

22.author: Mubarakpuri
22.text: And when he [Yusuf] attained his full manhood, We gave him wisdom and knowledge (the prophethood), thus We reward the doers of good.

22.author: Pickthall
22.text: And And when he reached his prime We gave him wisdom and knowledge. Thus We reward the good.

22.author: Qarai
22.text: When he came of age, We gave him judgement and [sacred] knowledge, and thus do We reward the virtuous.

22.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
22.text: And when he reached maturity, We bestowed on him judgment and knowledge. As such We recompense those who do good.

22.author: Saheeh International
22.text: And when Joseph reached maturity, We gave him judgment and knowledge. And thus We reward the doers of good.

22.author: Sarwar
22.text: When he attained maturity, God gave him strength, wisdom and knowledge. Thus, do We reward those who do good.

22.author: Shakir
22.text: And when he had attained his maturity, We gave him wisdom and knowledge: and thus do We reward those who do good.

22.author: Wahiduddin Khan
22.text: And when he reached maturity, We bestowed on him right judgement and knowledge. Thus We reward those who do good.

22.author: Yusuf Ali
22.text: When Joseph attained His full manhood, We gave him power and knowledge: thus do We reward those who do right.

22.author: Transliteration
22.text: Walamma balagha ashuddahu ataynahu hukman waAAilman wakathalika najzee almuhsineena

23.author: Ahmed Ali
23.text: But she in whose house he resided wished to seduce him and, closing the doors, said: "Come into me. "God forbid!" he said; "he is my master who has approved my stay. Surely those who act wrongly do not prosper."

23.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
23.text: And the woman in whose house he was, allured him not to restrain himself and she closed all the doors – and said, “Come! It is you I address!”; he said, “(I seek) The refuge of Allah – indeed the governor is my master – he treats me well; undoubtedly the unjust never prosper.”

23.author: Arberry
23.text: Now the woman in whose house he was solicited him, and closed the doors on them. 'Come,' she said, 'take me!' 'God be my refuge,' he said. 'Surely my lord has given me a goodly lodging. Surely the evildoers do not prosper.

23.author: Daryabadi
23.text: And she in whose house he was, solicited him against himself; and she fastened the doors, and said: come on, O thou! He said: Allah be my refuge: verily he is my lord; he hath made me a goodly dwelling; verily the wrong-doers fare not well.

23.author: Hilali & Khan
23.text: And she, in whose house he was, sought to seduce him (to do an evil act), she closed the doors and said: "Come on, O you." He said: "I seek refuge in Allah (or Allah forbid)! Truly, he (your husband) is my master! He made my stay agreeable! (So I will never betray him). Verily, the Zalimun (wrong and evil-doers) will never be successful."

23.author: Itani
23.text: She in whose house he was living tried to seduce him. She shut the doors, and said, “I am yours.” He said, “God forbid! He is my Lord. He has given me a good home. Sinners never succeed.”

23.author: Maududi
23.text: And it so happened that the lady in whose house Joseph was living, sought to tempt him to herself, and one day bolting the doors she said: "Come on now!" Joseph answered: "May Allah grant me refuge! My Lord has provided an honourable abode for me (so how can I do something so evil)? Such wrong-doers never prosper."

23.author: Mubarakpuri
23.text: And she, in whose house he was, sought to seduce him (to do an evil act), and she closed the doors and said: "Come on, O you." He said: "I seek refuge in Allah! Truly, he is my Rabb! He made my living in a great comfort! Verily, the wrongdoers will never be successful."

23.author: Pickthall
23.text: And she, in whose house he was, asked of him an evil act. She bolted the doors and said: Come! He said: I seek refuge in Allah! Lo! he is my lord, who hath treated me honourably. Lo! wrong-doers never prosper.

23.author: Qarai
23.text: The woman in whose house he was, solicited him. She closed the doors and said, ‘Come!!’ He said, ‘God forbid! Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a good abode. Indeed the wrongdoers are not felicitous.’

23.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
23.text: And she, in whose house he was, sought to seduce him and closed the doors saying: 'Come' 'In Allah is my refuge' he replied. 'My Lord has made my dwelling a good one. Harmdoers never prosper'

23.author: Saheeh International
23.text: And she, in whose house he was, sought to seduce him. She closed the doors and said, "Come, you." He said, "[I seek] the refuge of Allah. Indeed, he is my master, who has made good my residence. Indeed, wrongdoers will not succeed."

23.author: Sarwar
23.text: His master's wife then tried to seduce him. She locked the doors and said to him, "Come on." He said, "I seek refuge in God who has given me a good place of shelter. The unjust will certainly have no happiness."

23.author: Shakir
23.text: And she in whose house he was sought to make himself yield (to her), and she made fast the doors and said: Come forward. He said: I seek Allah's refuge, surely my Lord made good my abode: Surely the unjust do not prosper.

23.author: Wahiduddin Khan
23.text: However, the woman in whose house he lived, wanted to seduce him. One day she bolted the doors and said, "Come!" He replied, "God forbid! Truly [your husband] is my master and has treated me honourably. Wrongdoers certainly never prosper."

23.author: Yusuf Ali
23.text: But she in whose house he was, sought to seduce him from his (true) self: she fastened the doors, and said: "Now come, thou (dear one)!" He said: "Allah forbid! truly (thy husband) is my lord! he made my sojourn agreeable! truly to no good come those who do wrong!"

23.author: Transliteration
23.text: Warawadathu allatee huwa fee baytiha AAan nafsihi waghallaqati alabwaba waqalat hayta laka qala maAAatha Allahi innahu rabbee ahsana mathwaya innahu la yuflihu alththalimoona

24.author: Ahmed Ali
24.text: But the woman desired him, and he would have desired her but for the indication he received from his Lord. This was so that We may avert both evil and lechery from him, for he was one of Our chosen devotees.

24.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
24.text: And indeed the woman desired him; and he too would have desired her were he not to witness the sign of his Lord*; this is what We did, to turn away evil and lewdness from him; indeed He is one of Our chosen bondmen. (* Allah saved him and he never desired this immorality.)

24.author: Arberry
24.text: For she desired him; and he would have taken her, but that he saw the proof of his Lord. So was it, that We might turn away from him evil and abomination; he was one of Our devoted servants.

24.author: Daryabadi
24.text: And assuredly she besought him, and he would have besoughther were it not that he had seen the argument of his Lord. Thus We did, in order that We might avert from him all evil and indecency; verily he was of our bondmen single-hearted.

24.author: Hilali & Khan
24.text: And indeed she did desire him and he would have inclined to her desire, had he not seen the evidence of his Lord. Thus it was, that We might turn away from him evil and illegal sexual intercourse. Surely, he was one of Our chosen, guided slaves.

24.author: Itani
24.text: She desired him, and he desired her, had he not seen the proof of his Lord. It was thus that We diverted evil and indecency away from him. He was one of Our loyal servants.

24.author: Maududi
24.text: And she advanced towards him, and had Joseph not perceived a sign from his Lord he too would have advanced towards her. Thus was Joseph shown a sign from his Lord that We might avert from him all evil and indecency, for indeed he was one of Our chosen servants.

24.author: Mubarakpuri
24.text: And indeed she did desire him, and he would have inclined to her desire, had he not seen the evidence of his Lord. Thus it was, that We might turn away from him evil and immoval sins. Surely, he was one of Our Mukhlasin servants.

24.author: Pickthall
24.text: She verily desired him, and he would have desired her if it had not been that he saw the argument of his Lord. Thus it was, that We might ward off from him evil and lewdness. Lo! he was of Our chosen slaves.

24.author: Qarai
24.text: She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] had he not beheld the proof of his Lord. So it was, that We might turn away from him all evil and indecency. He was indeed one of Our dedicated servants.

24.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
24.text: She desired him, had he not been shown the proof of his Lord he would have taken her. But We turned him away from evil and abomination, for he was one of Our sincere worshipers.

24.author: Saheeh International
24.text: And she certainly determined [to seduce] him, and he would have inclined to her had he not seen the proof of his Lord. And thus [it was] that We should avert from him evil and immorality. Indeed, he was of Our chosen servants.

24.author: Sarwar
24.text: She was determined to have him and were it not for his faith in God, he would certainly have yielded to her. Thus did We protect him from evil and indecency. He was certainly one of Our sincere servants.

24.author: Shakir
24.text: And certainly she made for him, and he would have made for her, were it not that he had seen the manifest evidence of his Lord; thus (it was) that We might turn away from him evil and indecency, surely he was one of Our sincere servants.

24.author: Wahiduddin Khan
24.text: She started towards him, and he would have succumbed to her, if he had not seen a sign from his Lord -- We did this in order to keep evil and indecency away from him, for he was truly one of Our chosen servants.

24.author: Yusuf Ali
24.text: And (with passion) did she desire him, and he would have desired her, but that he saw the evidence of his Lord: thus (did We order) that We might turn away from him (all) evil and shameful deeds: for he was one of Our servants, sincere and purified.

24.author: Transliteration
24.text: Walaqad hammat bihi wahamma biha lawla an raa burhana rabbihi kathalika linasrifa AAanhu alssooa waalfahshaa innahu min AAibadina almukhlaseena

25.author: Ahmed Ali
25.text: Both of them raced to the door, and she (grabbed and) rent his shirt from behind. They met her lord outside the door. "There is no other penalty for a man," said she, "who wanted to outrage your wife but imprisonment or grievous punishment."

25.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
25.text: And they both raced towards the door, and the woman tore his shirt from behind, and they both found her husband at the door; she said, “What is the punishment of the one who sought evil with your wife, other than prison or a painful torture?”

25.author: Arberry
25.text: They raced to the door; and she tore his shirt from behind. They encountered her master by the door. She said, 'What is the recompense of him who purposes evil against thy folk, but that he should be imprisoned, or a painful chastisement?'

25.author: Daryabadi
25.text: And the twain raced to the door, and she rent his shirt from behind, And the twain met her master at the door. She said: What is the meed of him who intended evil toward thy house hold except that he be imprisoned, or a torment afflictive?

25.author: Hilali & Khan
25.text: So they raced with one another to the door, and she tore his shirt from the back. They both found her lord (i.e. her husband) at the door. She said: "What is the recompense (punishment) for him who intended an evil design against your wife, except that he be put in prison or a painful torment?"

25.author: Itani
25.text: As they raced towards the door, she tore his shirt from behind. At the door, they ran into her husband. She said, “What is the penalty for him who desired to dishonor your wife, except imprisonment or a painful punishment?”

25.author: Maududi
25.text: Then both of them rushed to the door, each seeking to get ahead of the other, and she tore Joseph's shirt from behind. Then both of them found the husband of the lady at the door. Seeing him she said: "What should be the punishment of him who has foul designs on your wife except that he should be imprisoned or subjected to painful chastisement?"

25.author: Mubarakpuri
25.text: So they raced with one another to the door, and she tore his shirt from the back. They both found her master (i.e. her husband) at the door. She said: "What is the recompense (punishment) for him who intended an evil design against your wife, except that he be put in prison or a painful torment"

25.author: Pickthall
25.text: And they raced with one another to the door, and she tore his shirt from behind, and they met her lord and master at the door. She said: What shall be his reward, who wisheth evil to thy folk, save prison or a painful doom?

25.author: Qarai
25.text: They raced to the door, and she tore his shirt from behind, and they ran into her husband at the door. She said, ‘What is to be the requital of him who has evil intentions for your wife except imprisonment or a painful punishment?’

25.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
25.text: They raced to the door and she tore his shirt from behind. And by the door, they met her husband whereupon she said: 'What is the recompense of one whose intent is evil against your people other than being imprisoned, or sternly punished'

25.author: Saheeh International
25.text: And they both raced to the door, and she tore his shirt from the back, and they found her husband at the door. She said, "What is the recompense of one who intended evil for your wife but that he be imprisoned or a painful punishment?"

25.author: Sarwar
25.text: She chased him to the door, grabbed him from behind, and tore off his shirt. Suddenly, they were face to face with her husband. (Looking accusingly at Joseph) she asked her husband, "What punishment is more fitting for those who have evil desires towards your household other than imprisonment and painful torment?"

25.author: Shakir
25.text: And they both hastened to the door, and she rent his shirt from behind and they met her husband at the door. She said: What is the punishment of him who intends evil to your wife except imprisonment or a painful chastisement?

25.author: Wahiduddin Khan
25.text: They both ran to the door and, in the struggle, she tore his shirt at the back. They found her husband at the door. She cried, "Shall not the man who wished to violate your wife be thrown into prison or sternly punished?"

25.author: Yusuf Ali
25.text: So they both raced each other to the door, and she tore his shirt from the back: they both found her lord near the door. She said: "What is the (fitting) punishment for one who formed an evil design against thy wife, but prison or a grievous chastisement?"

25.author: Transliteration
